explanation:
**Precision cooking** improves results. Portions, recipe scaling, and cooking times follow predictable patterns.

## How It Works

Complete vegan protein combinations pair foods with complementary amino acid profiles. Legumes are low in methionine but high in lysine. Grains are low in lysine but high in methionine. When combined, they provide all 9 essential amino acids in adequate amounts.

This calculator applies the formula using your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to potentially unusual values.

## Final Notes

This calculation is an informational reference. For critical decisions (medical, dietary, or health-related), consult with a qualified nutrition professional. Values updated in 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: A complete protein contains all 9 essential amino acids in sufficient quantities for human nutrition.
Most legumes (beans, lentils, peas) are incomplete—they're low in methionine but high in lysine.
Most grains are incomplete—they're low in lysine but high in methionine.

faq.q: How do I combine incomplete proteins?
faq.a: Pair legumes with grains in the same meal. The combination provides all 9 essential amino acids. Examples: lentils + rice, beans + corn, hummus + pita.

faq.q: Is soy a complete vegan protein?
faq.a: Yes, soybeans and tofu are complete proteins on their own—they contain all 9 essential amino acids.

faq.q: Is quinoa a complete vegan protein?
faq.a: Yes, quinoa is a complete plant protein with all 9 essential amino acids, making it unique among grains.
PDCAAS (Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score) measures protein quality. A score of 1.0 is perfect; higher scores indicate better amino acid availability.
